Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## STOCHASTIC INVERSE REINFORCEMENT LEARNING

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

The goal of the inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) problem is to recover the reward functions from expert demonstrations. However, the IRL problem like any ill-posed inverse problem suffers the congenital defect that the policy may be optimal for many reward functions, and expert demonstrations may be optimal for many policies. In this work, we generalize the IRL problem to a well-posed expectation optimization problem _stochastic inverse reinforcement learning_ (SIRL) to recover the probability distribution over reward functions. We adopt the Monte Carlo expectation-maximization (MCEM) method to estimate the parameter of the probability distribution as the first solution to the SIRL problem. The solution is _succinct, robust, and transferable_ for a learning task and can generate alternative solutions to the IRL problem. Through our formulation, it is possible to observe the intrinsic property for the IRL problem from a _global_ viewpoint, and our approach achieves a considerable performance on the _objectworld_ .

## 1 INTRODUCTION

The IRL problem addresses an inverse problem that a set of expert demonstrations determines a reward function over a Markov decision process (MDP) if the model dynamics are known Russell (1998); Ng et al. (2000). The recovered reward function provides a _succinct, robust, and transferable_ definition of the learning task and completely determines the optimal policy. However, the IRL problem is ill-posed that the policy may be optimal for many reward functions and expert demonstrations may be optimal for many policies. For example, all policies are optimal for a constant reward function. In a real-world scenario, experts always act sub-optimally or inconsistently, which is another challenge.

To overcome these limitations, two classes of probabilistic approaches for the IRL problem are proposed, i.e., Bayesian inverse reinforcement learning (BIRL) Ramachandran & Amir (2007) based on Bayesians’ maximum a posteriori (MAP) estimation and maximum entropy IRL (MaxEnt) Ziebart et al. (2008); Ziebart (2010) based on frequentists’ maximum likelihood (MLE) estimation. BIRL solves for the distribution of reward functions without an assumption that experts behave optimally, and encode the external _a priori_ information in a choice of a prior distribution. However, BIRL also suffers from the practical limitation that a large number of algorithmic iterations is required for the procedure of Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) in a sampling of posterior over reward functions. Advanced techniques, for example Kernel technique Michini & How (2012) and gradient method Choi & Kim (2011), are proposed to improve the efficiency and tractability of this situation.

MaxEnt employs the principle of maximum entropy to resolve the ambiguity in choosing demonstrations over a policy. This class of methods, inheriting the merits from previous non-probabilistic IRL approaches including Ng et al. (2000); Abbeel & Ng (2004); Ratliff et al. (2006); Abbeel et al. (2008); Syed & Schapire (2008); Ho et al. (2016), imposes regular structures of reward functions in a combination of hand-selected features. Formally, the reward function is a linear or nonlinear combination of the feature basis functions which consists of a set of real-valued functions _{φi_ ( _s, a_ ) _}i_ =1 hand-selected by experts. The goal of this approach is to find the best-fitting weights of feature basis functions through the MLE approach. Wulfmeier et al. (2015) and Levine et al. (2011) use deep neural networks and Gaussian processes to fit the parameters based on demonstrations respectively but still suffer from the problem that the true reward shaped by the changing environment dynamics.

Influenced by the work of Finn et al. (2016a;b), Fu et al. (2017) propose a framework called adversarial IRL (AIRL) to recover robust reward functions in a changing dynamics based on adversarial learning and achieves superior results. Compared with AIRL, another adversarial method called generative

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

adversarial imitation learning (GAIL) Ho & Ermon (2016) seeks to directly recover the expert’s policy rather than reward functions. Many follow-up methods enhance and extend GAIL for multipurpose in various application scenarios Li et al. (2017); Hausman et al. (2017); Wang et al. (2017). However, GAIL is in a lack of an explanation of expert’s behavior and a portable representation for the knowledge transfer which are the merits of the class of the MaxEnt approach, because the MaxEnt approach is equipped with the "transferable" regular structures over reward functions.

In this paper, under the framework of the MaxEnt approach, we propose a generalized perspective of studying the IRL problem called _stochastic inverse reinforcement learning_ (SIRL). It is formulated as an expectation optimization problem aiming to recover a probability distribution over the reward function from expert demonstrations. The solution of SIRL is _succinct_ and _robust_ for the learning task in the meaning that it can generate more than one weight over feature basis functions which compose alternative solutions to the IRL problem. Benefits of the class of the MaxEnt method, the solution to our generalized problem SIRL is also _transferable_ . Since of the intractable integration in our formulation, we employ the Monte Carlo expectation-maximization (MCEM) approach Wei & Tanner (1990) to give the first solution to the SIRL problem in a model-based environment. In general, the solutions to the IRL problem are not always best-fitting in the previous approaches because a highly nonlinear inverse problem with the limited information is very likely to get trapped in a secondary maximum in the recovery. Taking advantage of the Monte Carlo mechanism of a _global_ exhaustive search, our MCEM approach avoids the secondary maximum and theoretically convergent demonstrated by pieces of literature Caffo et al. (2005); Chan & Ledolter (1995). Our approach is also quickly convergent because of the preset simple geometric configuration over weight space in which we approximate it with a Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM). Hence, our approach works well in a real-world scenario with a small and variability set of expert demonstrations.

In particular, the contributions of this paper are threefold:

1. We generalize the IRL problem to a well-posed expectation optimization problem SIRL.

2. We provide the first theoretically existing solution to SIRL by the MCEM approach.

3. We show the effectiveness of our approach by comparing the performance of the proposed method to those of the previous algorithms on the _objectworld_ .

## 2 PRELIMINARY

An MDP is a tuple _M_ := _⟨S, A, T , R, γ⟩_ , where _S_ is the set of states, _A_ is the set of actions, and the transition function (a.k.a. model dynamics) _T_ := P( _st_ +1 = _s[′]_[��] _st_ = _s, at_ = _a_ ) for _s, s[′] ∈S_ and _a ∈A_ is the probability of being current state _s_ , taking action _a_ and yielding next state _s[′]_ . Reward function _R_ ( _s, a_ ) is a real-valued function and _γ ∈_ [0 _,_ 1) is the discount factor. A policy _π_ : _S →A_ is deterministic or stochastic, where the deterministic one is written as _a_ = _π_ ( _s_ ), and the stochastic one is as a conditional distribution _π_ ( _a|s_ ). Sequential decisions are recorded in a series of episodes which consist of states _s_ , actions _a_ , and rewards _r_ . The goal of reinforcement learning aims to get optimal policy _π[∗]_ for maximizing the expected total reward, i.e.

Given an MDP without a reward function _R_ , i.e., MDP _\_ R= _⟨S, A, T ⟩_ , and _m_ expert demonstrations _ζ[E]_ := _{ζ_[1] _, · · · , ζ[m] }_ . Each expert demonstration _ζ[i]_ is a sequential of state-action pairs. The goal of the IRL problem is to estimate the unknown reward function _R_ ( _s, a_ ) from expert demonstrations _ζ[E]_ and the reward functions compose a complete MDP. The estimated complete MDP yields an optimal policy that acts as closely as the expert demonstrations.

## 2.1 REGULAR STRUCTURE OF REWARD FUNCTIONS

In this section, we provide a formal definition of the regular (linear/nonlinear) structure of reward functions. The linear structure Ng et al. (2000); Ziebart et al. (2008); Syed & Schapire (2008); Ho

et al. (2016) is an _M_ linear combination of feature basis functions, written as,

where _φi_ : _S × A �−→_ R _[d]_ are a _d_ -dimensional feature functions hand-selected by experts.

The nonlinear structure Wulfmeier et al. (2015) is of the form as follows,
where _N_ is neural networks of hand-crafted reward feature basis functions _{φi_ ( _s, a_ ) _}[M] i_ =1[.]
In the following section, we propose an optimization problem whose solution is a probability distribution over weights of basis functions _{φi_ ( _s, a_ ) _}i_ =1.

## 2.2 PROBLEM STATEMENT

Given an MDP _\R_ := ( _S, A, T , γ_ ) with a known transition function _T_ := P( _st_ +1 = _s[′]_[��] _st_ = _s, at_ = _a_ ) for _s, s[′] ∈S_ and _a ∈A_ and a hand-crafted reward feature basis function _{φi_ ( _s_ ) _}[M] i_ =1[.][A stochastic] regular structure on the reward function assumes weights _W_ of the reward feature functions _φi_ ( _s_ ), which are random variables with a reward conditional probability distribution _D[R]_ ( _W|ζ[E]_ ) conditional on expert demonstrations _ζ[E]_ . Parametrizing _D[R]_ ( _W|ζ[E]_ ) with parameter Θ, our aim is to estimate the best-fitting parameter Θ _[∗]_ from the expert demonstrations _ζ[E]_ , such that _D[R]_ ( _W|ζ[E] ,_ Θ _[∗]_ ) more likely generates weights to compose reward functions as the ones derived from expert demonstrations, which is called _stochastic inverse reinforcement learning_ problem.

Suppose a representative trajectory class _Cϵ[E]_[satisfies that each trajectory element set] _[ O][∈C] ϵ[E]_[is a] subset of expert demonstrations _ζ[E]_ with the cardinality at least _ϵ · |ζ[E] |_ , where _ϵ_ is a preset threshold and _|ζ[E] |_ is the number of expert demonstrations, written as,
Integrate out unobserved weights _W_ , and the SIRL problem is formulated to estimate parameter Θ on an expectation optimization problem over the representative trajectory class as follows:

where trajectory element set _O_ assumes to be uniformly distributed for the sake of simplicity in this study, and _fM_ is the conditional joint probability density function of trajectory element _O_ and weights _W_ for reward feature functions conditional on parameter Θ.

## 2.2.1 NOTE:

- Problem 1 is well-posed and typically not intractable analytically.

- Trajectory element set _O_ is usually known from the rough estimation of the statistics in expert demonstrations in practice.

- We introduce _representative trajectory class_ to overcome the limitation of the sub-optimality of expert demonstrations _ζ[E]_ Ramachandran & Amir (2007), which is also called a lack of sampling representativeness in statistics Kruskal & Mosteller (1979), e.g., driver’s demonstrations encode his own preferences in driving style but may not reflect the true rewards of an environment. With the technique of representative trajectory class, each subset of driver’s demonstrations (i.e. trajectory element set _O_ ) constitutes a subproblem in Problem 1.

## 3 METHODOLOGY

In this section, we propose a novel approach to estimate the best-fitting parameter Θ _[∗]_ in Problem 1, which is called the two-stage hierarchical method, _a variant of MCEM_ method.

## 3.2 TERMINATION CRITERIA

In this section, we discuss the termination criteria in our algorithm. EM terminates usually when the parameters do not substantively change after enough iterations. For example, one classic termination criterion in EM terminates at the _t_ -th iteration satisfying as follows,

for user-specified _δEM_ and _ϵEM_ , where _θ_ is the model parameter in EM.

However, the same termination criterion for MCEM has a risk of early terminating because of the Monte Carlo error in the update step. Hence, we adopt a practical method in which the following stopping criterion holds in three consecutive times,
for user-specified _δMCEM_ and _ϵMCEM_ Booth & Hobert (1999). Other stopping criteria for MCEM refers to Caffo et al. (2005); Chan & Ledolter (1995).

## 3.3 CONVERGENCE ISSUE

The convergence issue of MCEM is more complicated than EM. In light of model-based interactive MDP _\_ R, we can always increase the sample size during each iteration of MCEM. In practice, we require the Monte Carlo sample size satisfy the following inequality,
Additional requirement for the convergence property is a compact assumption over that parameter space. A comprehensive proof refers to Chan & Ledolter (1995); Fort et al. (2003).

The pseudocode is given in Appendix.

## 4 EXPERIMENTS

We evaluate our approach on a classic environment _objectworld_ introduced by Levine et al. (2011) which is a particularly challenging environment with a large number of irrelevant features and the highly nonlinearity of the reward functions. Note that since almost only _objectworld_ provides a tool that allows analysis and display the evolution procedure of the SIRL problem in a 2D heat map, we skip the typical invisible physics-based control tasks for the evaluation of our approach, i.e. cartpole Barto et al. (1983), mountain car Moore (1990), MuJoCo Todorov et al. (2012), and etc.
We employ the expected value difference (EVD) proposed by Levine et al. (2011) to be the metric of optimality as follows:

which is a measure of the difference between the expected reward earned under the optimal policy _π[∗]_ , given by the true reward, and the policy derived from the rewards sampling from our reward conditional probability distribution _D_ ( _W|ζ[E] ,_ Θ _[∗]_ ), where Θ _[∗]_ is the best estimation parameter in our approach.

## 4.1 OBJECTWORLD

The _objectworld_ is a learning environment for the IRL problem. It is an _N × N_ grid board with colored objects placed in randomly selected cells. Each colored object is assigned one inner color and one outer color from _C_ preselected colors. Each cell on the grid board is a state, and stepping to four neighbor cells (up, down, left, right) or staying in place (stay) are five actions with a 30% chance of moving in a random direction.

The ground truth of the reward function is defined in the following way. Suppose two primary colors of _C_ preselected colors are red and blue. The reward of a state is 1 if the state is within 3 steps of an outer red object and 2 steps of an outer blue object, -1 if the state is within 3 steps of an outer red object, and 0 otherwise. The other pairs of inner and outer colors are distractors. Continuous and discrete versions of feature basis functions are provided. For the continuous version, _φ_ ( _s_ ) is a 2 _C_ -dimensional real-valued feature vector. Each dimension records the Euclidean distance from the state to objects. For example, the first and second coordinates are the distances to the nearest inner and outer red object respectively, and so on through all _C_ colors. For the discrete version, _φ_ ( _s_ ) is a (2 _C · N_ )-dimensional binary feature vector. Each _N_ -dimensional vector records a binary representation of the distance to the nearest inner or outer color object with the _d_ -th coordinate 1 if the corresponding continuous distance is less than _d_ .

## 4.2 EVALUATION PROCEDURE AND ANALYSIS

In this section, we design three tasks to evaluate the effectiveness of our generative model _reward conditional probability distribution D_ ( _W|ζ[E] ,_ Θ _[∗]_ ). For each task, the environment setting is as follows. The instance of a 10 _×_ 10 _objectworld_ has 25 random objects with 2 colors and a 0.9 discount factor. 20 expert demonstrations are generated according to the given optimal policy for the

6

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

recovery. The length of each expert demonstration is 5-grid size trajectory length. Four algorithms for the evaluation includes MaxEnt, DeepMaxEnt, SIRL, and DSIRL, where SIRL and DSIRL are implemented as Algorithm 2 in Appendix. The weights are drawn from reward conditional probability distribution _D_ ( _W|ζ[E] ,_ Θ _[∗]_ ) as the coefficients of feature basis functions _{φi_ ( _s, a_ ) _}i_ =1.

In our evaluation, SIRL and DSIRL start from 10 samples and double the sample size per iteration until it converges for the convergence issue, refer to Section 3.2. In the first stage, the epochs of algorithm iteration are set to 20 and the learning rates are 0.01. The parameter _ϵ_ in representative trajectory set _Oϵ[E]_[is preset as 0.95.][In the second stage, 3-component GMM for SIRL and DSIRL is] set with at most 1000 iterations before convergence. Additionally, the architecture of neural networks in DeepMaxEnt and DSIRL are implemented as 3-layer fully-connected with the sigmoid function.

## 4.2.1 EVALUATION PLATFORM

All the methods are implemented in Python 3.5 and Theano 1.0.0 with a machine learning distributed framework _Ray_ Moritz et al. (2018). The experiments are conducted on a machine with Intel(R) Core(TM) i7-7700 CPU @ 3.60GHz and Nvidia GeForce GTX 1070 GPU.

## 4.2.2 RECOVERY EXPERIMENT

In the recovery experiment, we compare the true reward function, the optimal policy, and the optimal value with ones derived from expert demonstrations under the four methods. Since our approach is an average of all the outcomes which are prone to imitate the true optimal value from expert demonstrations, we use the mean of reward conditional probability distribution for SIRL and DSIRL as a comparison.

In Figure 1, the EVD of the optimal values in the last row are 48.9, 31.1, 33.7 and 11.3 for four methods respectively, and the covariances of GMM model for SIRL and DSIRL are limited up to 5.53 and 1.36 on each coordinate respectively. It yields that in a highly nonlinear inverse problem, the recovery abilities of SIRL and DISRL are better than MaxEnt’s and DeepMaxEnt’s respectively. The reason is mainly because Monte Carlo mechanism in our approach alleviates the problem of getting stuck in local minima by allowing random exit from it.

## 4.2.3 ROBUSTNESS EXPERIMENT

In the robustness experiment, we evaluate the robustness of our approach that solutions generated by _D_ ( _W|ζ[E] ,_ Θ _[∗]_ ) are effective to the IRL problem. To capture the robust solutions, we design the following generative algorithm with the pseudocode in Algorithm 1.

**Figure 1:** Results for recovery experiment.

**Figure 2:** Results for the robustness experiment.

In the right generative algorithm, we use Frobenius norm to measure the distance between weights drawn from _D_ ( _W|ζ[E] ,_ Θ _[∗]_ ) as follows,
We also constrain that each drawn weight _W ∼D_ ( _W|ζ[E] ,_ Θ _[∗]_ ) in the solution set _G_ satisfies as follows,
where _W[′]_ represents any member in the solution set _G_ . _δ, ϵ_ are the preset thresholds in the generative algorithm.

## **Algorithm 1:** Generative Algorithm

**Input:** _D_ ( _W|ζ[E] ,_ Θ _[∗]_ ), required solution set size _N_ , and preset thresholds _ϵ_ and _δ_ . **Output:** Solution set _G_ := _{Wi}[N] i_ =1[.]
**while** _i < N_ **do** _W ∼D_ ( _W|ζ[E] ,_ Θ _[∗]_ ) ; **for** _any W[′] ∈S_ **do if** _||W −W[′] ||F > δ and EVD_ ( _W_ ) _< ϵ_ **then** _G ←W_ ; **end end** _i ← i_ + 1; **end**

In Figure 2, the right column figures are generated from weights in the solution set _G_ whose EVD values are around 10.2. Note that the recovered reward function in the first row has a similar but different pattern appearance. The optimal value derived from these recovered reward functions has a very small EVD value with the true reward. It yields the effectiveness of our _robust_ generative model which can generate more than one solutions to the IRL problem.

## 4.2.4 HYPERPARAMETER EXPERIMENT

In the hyperparameter experiment, we evaluate the effectiveness of our approach under the influence of different preset quantities and qualities of expert demonstrations. The amount of information carried in expert demonstrations composes a specific learning environment, and hence it has an impact on the effectiveness of our generative model. We verify three hyperparameters including the number of expert demonstrations in Figure 3, the trajectory length of expert demonstrations in Figure 4 and the portion size in representative trajectory class _Cϵ[E]_[in Figure][ 5][ on the] _[ objectworld]_[.][The] shadow of the line in the figures represents the standard error for each experimental trail. Notice that the EVDs for SIRL and DSIRL are both decreasing as the number and the trajectory length of expert demonstrations, and the portion size in the representative trajectory class are increasing. A notable point in Figure 3 is that very few expert demonstrations (less than 200) for our approach also yields a small EVDs, which manifests the merit of Monte Carlo mechanism in our approach.

**Figure 3:** Results under 40, 80, 160, 320, 640, 1280 and 2560 expert demonstrations.

**Figure 4:** Results under 1, 2, 4, 8, **Figure 5:** Results under 0.65, 16, 32, and 64 grid size trajectory 0.70, 0.75, 0.80, 0.85, 0.90, and length of expert demonstrations. 0.95 portion size in _Cϵ[E]_[.]

## 5 CONCLUSION

In this paper, we propose a generalized problem SIRL for the IRL problem to get the distribution of reward functions. The new problem is well-posed and we employ the method of MCEM to give the first _succinct, robust, and transferable_ solution. In the experiment, we evaluate our approach on the _objectworld_ and the experimental results confirm the effectiveness of our approach.

zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback: The paper introduces Stochastic Inverse Reinforcement Learning (SIRL), a framework that treats reward functions as random variables and utilizes a Monte Carlo Expectation-Maximization (MCEM) approach to estimate the distribution of these functions. While the conceptual move toward a probabilistic distribution of rewards is a compelling way to handle the ill-posed nature of IRL, the paper suffers from significant gaps in mathematical rigor, particularly in the derivation of the MCEM steps. The presentation of the expectation updates and the transition from the general SIRL optimization to the algorithmic steps is difficult to follow. Despite these technical shortcomings, the empirical evaluation on the Objectworld domain provides some evidence for the utility of this approach.

comments.title: Unjustified step in the decomposition of the joint density
comments.quote:
The conditional joint density $f_M ( O, W| \Theta)$ in Equation 1 can be written as the product of two conditional densities $g_M$ and $h_M$ as follows:

$$f_M(O, W | \Theta) = g_M(O | W, \Theta_1) \cdot h_M(W | \zeta_E, \Theta_2)$$
comments.explanation: The paper transitions from an optimization problem involving $f_M(O, W | \Theta)$ to a two-stage hierarchical method by claiming $f_M$ can be written as the product of $g_M$ and $h_M$. This implies a conditional independence assumption (that $O$ is independent of $\Theta_2$ given $W$ and $\Theta_1$, and $W$ is independent of $\Theta_1$ given $\zeta_E$ and $\Theta_2$). This is a non-trivial assumption that is not justified. While one can factorize joint densities, the specific dependency on the partition of $\Theta$ into $\Theta_1$ and $\Theta_2$ is not explained, leaving the validity of the subsequent maximization steps in Equations 4 and 5 uncertain.

comments.title: Notation confusion in the EM update
comments.explanation: The notation becomes difficult to parse here. The subscript $t_i$ appears in the superscript of $g_M$, but it is not clear if this refers to the iteration $t$ or something else. Furthermore, $W_i^{\Theta_t}$ is used as the input to the reward function. Given that $W$ is defined as the weight variable for feature basis functions, using it as a fixed argument in $g_M$ inside the maximization step for $\Theta_1$ makes the definition of the 'model parameter' $\Theta_1$ circular. It is unclear if $\Theta_1$ refers to parameters of the transition dynamics (which are assumed known in the preliminaries) or something else entirely.

comments.title: Mathematical Ambiguity in GMM E-step
comments.explanation: The text refers to $W_i^{\Theta_t}$ as a 'm-step reward weight', which, based on the previous section, seems to be a point estimate obtained via gradient ascent. However, the E-step of an EM algorithm for a GMM usually involves the probability (responsibility) of an assignment based on the actual samples. If these weights $W_i^{\Theta_t}$ are the samples themselves, the variable indexing is confusing. The notation does not clarify whether $W_i^{\Theta_t}$ in the E-step is a single sample or a distribution. This makes the implementation of the second stage of the hierarchical method underspecified.

zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Zero Shot (deepseek-v4-flash)
zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper proposes a stochastic generalization of inverse reinforcement learning (SIRL) using a Monte Carlo EM approach, aiming to recover a distribution over reward functions. While the motivation is interesting, the methodological exposition is critically flawed. The factorization of the joint density confuses the conditioning on expert demonstrations, and the first-stage update contradicts itself: the text describes a Monte Carlo maximization of an empirical expectation but then claims to compute separate parameters per sample, conflating the reward weights W with the parameter Θ1. These issues make the algorithm ambiguous and non-reproducible. The experimental evaluation is limited to a single environment and does not compensate for the theoretical shortcomings.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency in joint density factorization
comments.explanation: The left side conditions only on Θ, but the right side conditions on ζ^E in h_M. Since the overall model should condition on the expert demonstrations ζ^E, the left side should include ζ^E explicitly (e.g., f_M(O,W|ζ^E,Θ)). This omission makes the derivation ambiguous and could mislead readers about the underlying probabilistic model.

comments.title: Contradiction between first-stage update equation and text
comments.quote: The parameter Θ1^{t+1} in Equation 6 has N_t coordinates written as Θ1^{t+1} := (Θ1^{t+1})_1, ..., (Θ1^{t+1})_{N_t}. For each learning task M_i^t, the i-th coordinate (Θ1^{t+1})_i is derived from maximization of a posteriori, (Θ1^{t+1})_i := arg max_θ log g_{M_i^t}(O_i^t | W_i^{Θ^t}, θ).
comments.explanation: Equation (6) defines Θ1^{t+1} as the argument maximizing a sum over i of log g, which would yield a single parameter vector. The text immediately contradicts this by claiming Θ1^{t+1} has N_t coordinates, each from a separate maximization on a single sample. This inconsistency makes the algorithmic step irreproducible and suggests a fundamental confusion between performing MAP estimation per sample and maximizing a Monte Carlo expectation.

comments.title: Ambiguous role of parameters: Θ1 vs. reward weights W
comments.quote: Hence, the parameter Θ1^{t+1} is represented as (W_m^{Θ_1^t}, ..., W_m^{Θ_{N_t}^t}).
comments.explanation: In the factorization, Θ1 is introduced as a parameter of the conditional density g_M(O|W,Θ1). But here Θ1^{t+1} is identified with a set of updated reward weights, which were originally latent variables W. This conflates the latent variable (to be integrated out) with the parameter of interest. The derivation no longer respects the original probabilistic model, and it is unclear what quantity g_M is supposed to represent.

comments.title: Underspecified sampling from representative trajectory class
comments.quote: Trajectory element set O assumes to be uniformly distributed for the sake of simplicity in this study, and O_i^t [uniformly drawn] from representative trajectory class C_ϵ^E
comments.explanation: The representative trajectory class C_ϵ^E is defined as all subsets O of ζ^E with |O| ≥ ϵ|ζ^E|. Sampling uniformly from such a huge class is non-trivial; the paper does not specify how to efficiently sample (e.g., by selecting a random subset of a fixed size, or by some other procedure). This omission hinders reproducibility.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim of well-posedness
comments.quote: We generalize the IRL problem to a well-posed expectation optimization problem SIRL.
comments.explanation: The paper asserts that the SIRL problem is 'well-posed' but provides no proof or argument about existence, uniqueness, or stability of solutions. In the context of ill-posed inverse problems, such a claim requires justification; without it, the statement is an overclaim.

comments.title: Undefined symbols in termination criteria
comments.explanation: The symbols δ_EM and ϵ_EM are introduced without definition or typical values. While the general idea of checking parameter change is clear, the lack of specification makes the termination condition ambiguous.

comments.title: Index inconsistency in weight update equation
comments.quote: W_m^{Θ_i^t} = W_{i-1}^{Θ_i^t} + λ_i^t ∇_W log g_{M_i^t}(O_i^t | W_i^{Θ^t}, ...)
comments.explanation: The gradient is taken with respect to W, but the argument inside log g uses W_i^{Θ^t} (the sampled weight), while the left side uses W_m^{Θ_i^t} with subscript m. The update step index is unclear and does not match the usual gradient ascent notation. This could be a typesetting issue but remains confusing.

zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.7-flash)
zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: The paper introduces Stochastic Inverse Reinforcement Learning (SIRL), which reformulates IRL as an expectation optimization problem over a distribution of reward functions. The proposed two-stage hierarchical MCEM method aims to recover this distribution, specifically estimating a Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) of reward weights. Experiments on the Objectworld benchmark demonstrate that SIRL achieves competitive sample efficiency and robustness compared to existing MaxEnt and DeepMaxEnt baselines. While the core idea is novel, the mathematical formulation in the Methodology section contains confusing notation regarding the optimization variables in the first stage, and several key theoretical definitions are missing or ambiguously defined in the provided text.

comments.title: Conflicting Optimization Variable in First Stage Update
comments.quote:
For each learning task $M[t]_i$ , the i-th coordinate (Θ[t]_1[t][+1])_i is derived from maximization of a posteriori, (Θ[t]_1[t][+1])_i := \arg max _	heta log _gMti \_ \Oi[t][|W] i_[\Theta][t][, 	heta] \_ ,
which is a convex formulation maximized by a gradient ascent method.

In practice, we move m steps uphill to the optimum in each learning task $M[t]_i$ . The update formula of m-step reward weights $W[m]_[\Theta] i_[t]$ is written as

$W[m]_[\Theta] i_[t] = W[i][t] + m \cdot \lambda \cdot 
abla_	heta log _gM(O[i][t] | W[i]_[\Theta][t], 	heta)$
comments.explanation:
In the first stage of the proposed MCEM algorithm, the authors introduce an optimization variable $	heta$ inside the function $\log g_M(O|W, 	heta)$ and compute a gradient with respect to $	heta$ ($
abla_	heta$). However, in the standard MaxEnt IRL framework with known transition dynamics (as defined in Section 2.1) and hand-crafted features, the probability of a trajectory $P(O|W)$ is fully determined by the reward weights $W$ itself; there are no additional free parameters $	heta$ to optimize. If $	heta$ is intended to represent the argument of the gradient (i.e., the weight vector $W$), the notation is incorrect; it should be $
abla_W$. If $	heta$ is meant to be a neural network parameter (as hinted at in the experiments section with DeepMaxEnt), updating $W$ via $
abla_	heta$ is mathematically invalid without a chain rule context. This presents a logical gap and a notation error that could mislead a reader attempting to reproduce the algorithm.

comments.title: Ambiguous Representative Trajectory Set Parameter $\epsilon$
comments.quote: The parameter $\epsilon$ in representative trajectory set $O\epsilon^E$ is preset as 0.95.
comments.explanation: In the definition of the problem in Section 2.2, the representative trajectory class $C_\epsilon^E$ is used to define a subset of expert demonstrations $O$. While $\epsilon$ is commonly used as a coverage threshold or ratio in statistical sampling (e.g., 0.1 or 0.5), setting it to 0.95 implies retaining nearly all trajectories from the demonstration set. This phrasing is ambiguous regarding the role of $O$—whether it is a random subset or a fixed feature-set representative—and makes it unclear how this helps overcome 'sub-optimality' or 'inconsistency' of expert demonstrations (claimed in Section 2.2.1). A more precise definition or justification for this high value is needed to understand the weighting of trajectories in the objective function.

comments.title: Missing Convergence Inequality Formula
comments.quote: In practice, we require the Monte Carlo sample size satisfy the following inequality,\n\nAdditional requirement for the convergence property is a compact assumption over that parameter space.
comments.explanation: Section 3.3 discusses the convergence requirements of the MCEM method but omits the actual mathematical inequality that must be satisfied by the Monte Carlo sample size. Without this formula, readers cannot verify the theoretical convergence claims or implement the condition correctly.

comments.title: OCR Notation Inconsistency in Sampling Step
comments.explanation: While the content is recoverable from context (sampling weights from the distribution conditioned on expert demos and the GMM parameter $\Theta_2$), the OCR output for the sampling notation is significantly corrupted. The mix of superscripts, subscripts, and specific array formatting in the set definition makes it difficult to parse the exact sampling procedure described in Section 3.1.1 without guessing the intended LaTeX structure.
